On Thu, May 7, 2026 at 12:16 PM Jordan Luna > wrote:
We can knock this out today. On your end I just need you to follow these instructions and copy/paste the DKIM records and give them to us (from google guides):

Important: In Google Workspace, after you turn on Gmail for your organization, you must wait 24–72 hours before you can get your DKIM key in the Admin console. If you try to generate a key before this time, you might get an error that the DKIM record was not created.

In the Google Admin console, go to Menu and then Apps and then Google Workspace and then Gmail.

1.
Go to Gmail
2.
Click Authenticate email.
3.
In the Selected domain menu, select the domain where you want to set up DKIM.
4.
Click the Generate New Record button.
5.
In the Generate new record box, select your DKIM key settings:

        DKIM key bit length options:
         2048—If your domain provider supports 2048-bit keys, select this option. Longer keys are more secure than shorter keys. If you previously used a 1024-bit key, you             can switch to a 2048-bit key if your domain provider supports them. 1024—If your domain host doesn't support 2048-bit keys, select this option.
        Prefix selector options:
        The default prefix selector is google. If you are using Google Workspace, this is the recommended option.
        If your domain already uses a DKIM key with the prefix google, enter a different prefix in this field. Read more about DKIM selectors.
   6. Click Generate. On the Authenticate email page, the TXT record value is updated and this message appears: DKIM authentication settings updated.

Important: The Authenticate email page in your Google Admin console might continue to display this message for up to 48 hours: You must update the DNS records for this domain. If you've correctly added your DKIM key at your domain provider, you can ignore this message.

Copy the DKIM values shown in the Authenticate email window

If this seems a bit much, I can always hop on your computer and take care of it. Just need to be logged in as an administrator to make these changes.

Jordan Luna

A28 Technical Solutions, Inc
Networks // Security // Web // Tech Support
________________________________
From: David Salter >
Sent: Thursday, May 7, 2026 12:08 PM
To: Assaf Lotan >; Jordan Luna >
Subject: Need Some Assistance

Assaf and Jordan

I am using an AI Agent to do marketing and sending emails to clients
When I send a test email to myself I receive a warning upon opening it, indicating it is suspicious and requires confirmation that it is safe
I clearly don't want that happening when emailing prospective clients

When I researched why that message appears , the response I got was

I checked public DNS for drs-engineering.net (http://drs-engineering.net/):

• Google MX exists ✅
• SPF record: missing
• DMARC record: missing
• DKIM record: not found for common Google selectors

That means Gmail has weak/no proof that mail from DRS@DRS-Engineering.net is legitimately authorized by the DRS domain. So Gmail may throw a warning and put it in spam, especially with:

• invoice/payment wording
• a newly embedded image signature
• automated/API-sent email
• low prior trust from that sender/domain

The fix is domain email authentication.

At minimum, in Wix DNS for drs-engineering.net , add:

SPF

v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all

DMARC

v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:DRS@DRS-Engineering.net 

DKIM
This must be generated inside Google Workspace Admin. Don’t guess it. Google will provide a TXT record like:

google._domainkey.drs-engineering.net 
v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=...

Best path:

1. Enable/generate DKIM in Google Workspace Admin.
2. Add SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records in Wix DNS.
3. Wait for DNS propagation.
4. Send another test.
5. If clean, then PlanHubGuy/client emails are much safer.

I have no idea what this means or how to do it. Can you please help.

Thanks
Dave Salter BSc. PhD PE
Founder and Principal
[https://ci3.googleusercontent.com/mail-sig/AIorK4wGq6J04wx4qxa7HtZyRD3aCMU3KjxmSQdqelLxL4q0MivYX2qARVXAT07YsWI4pq2Up5AyXND6E0B6]

Good morning Melanie,

Thank you for the comments.

Regarding Section 5.1, we agree to reinstate the second sentence, provided
the proportional negligence language is added.

Given the size of this project, we prefer to limit our liability to our
available insurance coverage. If that limitation is not acceptable, we
would need to adjust the project fee to $20,000.

Thanks.

On Tue, Jul 7, 2026 at 10:16 AM Melanie Heilig 
wrote:

And here were the comment on the insurance redlines. Highlighted yellow is
the most recent comment.

DRS: “Most of our comments are intended to align the agreement with DRS’s
role on the project as a professional consultant, not a contractor
performing construction work.“

- Professional consultants have commercial general liability insurance
exposures as well as professional liability insurance exposures.
Professionals need both insurances to be sure their work is properly
insured.

DRS: “DRS will not be performing construction activities, controlling means
and methods, or maintaining an on-site construction presence.“

- If DRS is providing the required CGL coverage, this is already
provided in the standard CG 0001 form that is required in 2.1

DRS: “We also marked up several insurance provisions that do not match
DRS’s operations or available coverage.”

- The required insurance in Exhibit D is easily available to
professional consultants.

- *2.1.6 Explosion, collapse, and underground hazards, including
subsidence: *

- LP: REJECT: This is a standard coverage built into the CGL form and
should not be an issue unless Consultant's policy includes an endorsement
that specifically excludes. If necessary, an alternative would be to add
"if the exposure exists" at the end of the sentence, if Melanie is
comfortable no exposure exists.
- DRS: Does not carry explosion, collapse, and underground hazard
coverage, including subsidence, because those exposures relate to
construction operations rather than the professional consulting
services we
will provide.
- PB: ACCEPT per Toby
- ACCEPT BUT IS NOT IDEAL: however, standard CG 0001 coverage as
required per 2.1 includes this coverage. If his policy is set up
otherwise, this raises concerns of his/his advisor’s insurance/risk
management credibility.

- *2.1.8: “Defense costs and expenses shall not reduce the limits of
insurance and shall not apply within any aggregate limit of insurance.
Defense within limits is not permitted for CGL and umbrella policies.
Defense costs shall not erode limits.*

- LP: REJECT: this is not acceptable. Consultant has a residual CGL
exposure and defense costs should be outside the limits of his
policy. If
his policy is set up otherwise, this raises concerns of his
insurance/risk
management credibility.
- PB: consider limit reduction per Toby.
- ACCEPT- BUT NOT IDEAL AND only if DRS provides evidence of at least
a $2M per occurrence limit.

- *3.1 Business Auto Liability Insurance (labeled as 2.1 Coverage):*

- LP: NEEDS EXPLANATION: if the Consultant entity does not own any
autos, then the "owned" auto requirement does not apply. If it does own
such autos but insures them elsewhere, it should still provide
evidence of
"owned" auto insurance.
- DRS: does not own company vehicles and therefore cannot provide
owned-auto coverage.
- ACCEPT- BUT NOT IDEAL, which is to leave the requirement as is and
accept DRS’ current evidence of insurance without “owned auto” coverage.

- *5.1.2: Contractors Pollution Liability Insurance*

- LP: REJECT: this requirement only applies if Consultant's work creates
a liability exposure that must be insured by a pollution policy. If
Melanie is confident this exposure does not exist, then Melanie
can approve
Consultant's evidence of insurance without requiring Consultant provide
evidence of pollution insurance. If not or if the pollution exposure can
arise during Consultant's performance of the PSA, then Consultant becomes
obligated to provide this insurance. Do not delete the requirement.
- DRS: DRS also does not carry contractor’s pollution liability
coverage, and our scope does not include hazardous materials handling,
hauling, or other pollution-generating operations.
- PB: ACCEPT per Toby
- ACCEPT: but ideal is to leave the requirement as is and accept DRS’
current evidence of insurance without CPL coverage.

- *6.2 No Limitation on Consultant’s Liability*

- LP: REJECT: by design the indemnity provisions are broader than
insurance requirements. This would effectively cap Consultant's exposure
to insured limits and exposures only. Unless negotiations require, we
suggest this proposed edit be rejected.
- There is no reason to delete this requirement.

*From:* Melanie Heilig
*Sent:* Monday, July 6, 2026 3:47 PM
*To:* 'Luke Bennett' 
*Cc:* Jody Robinson 
*Subject:* RE: DRS Engineering Rockfall fence Agreement redlines

I think we are close. Do you guys love contract negotiations as much as I
do lol?!

Our legal team had one comment on 5.1

On the indemnity in Article 5.1, if they are requesting indemnity to DRS’s
proportional negligence, then the language they struck in the second/last
sentence must remain. They would still be obligated to indemnify/defend
Plenium to the extent of DRS’s negligence and that obligation would not be
eliminated based on Plenium (or other person to be indemnified) were also
partially at fault. They cannot have both though. So, you can give them the
insert at the end of the first sentence, but the second sentence must
remain as-is.

Are you okay with us adding the second sentence back in?

Melanie

*From:* Luke Bennett 
*Sent:* Monday, July 6, 2026 2:30 PM
*To:* Melanie Heilig 
*Cc:* Jody Robinson 
*Subject:* Re: DRS Engineering Rockfall fence Agreement redlines

Good afternoon Melanie,
Thank you for the prompt response. Please see attached updated redlines for
your review.
Most of our comments are intended to align the agreement with DRS’s role on
the project as a professional consultant, not a contractor performing
construction work. DRS will not be performing construction activities,
controlling means and methods, or maintaining an on-site construction
presence. With that in mind, we revised the indemnity language so that
DRS’s obligations apply to claims directly arising from our services and
only to the extent of DRS’s proportional negligence.
We also marked up several insurance provisions that do not match DRS’s
operations or available coverage. Specifically, DRS does not own company
vehicles and therefore cannot provide owned-auto coverage. DRS also does
not carry contractor’s pollution liability coverage, and our scope does not
include hazardous materials handling, hauling, or other
pollution-generating operations. Similarly, DRS does not carry explosion,
collapse, and underground hazard coverage, including subsidence, because
those exposures relate to construction operations rather than the
professional consulting services we will provide.
Please let us know if you would like to discuss any of these edits. We are
happy to work through the language so the agreement accurately reflects
DRS’s scope, responsibilities, and insurance program.

On Mon, Jul 6, 2026 at 11:35 AM Melanie Heilig 
wrote:

Thank you for sending this. Our legal team has sent the following comments
in response to your redlines. Let me know if your thoughts on these
comments.

5.1. Reject deletion of defend (that must stay). Can agree to “directly”
instead of “arising out or in any way” but will then reject the third edit
at the end of the paragraph (our language will stay).

Article 9 applies for taxes that “may be payable” and license, etc. “which
may be necessary” – so only as applicable. Under 9.1, I am sure there are
some form of taxes required “of whatever nature” and to the extent there
are any such taxes, DRS needs to cover them and hold us harmless – if there
are none, then fine and that does not/would not apply (so shouldn’t be an
issue for them). Under 9.2, they certainly need licensure. If they are not
going to be responsible for permits, plan checks, and certificates of
inspection, then those can be carved out, but not the hold paragraph – but,
again, it is as “may be necessary” / as applicable, so if permits are not
necessary/applicable, then they are not covered already.

Exhibit D – I am attaching comments from our insurance provider. With
regards to 2.1.6 and 5.1.2, I do not see any risk here so I think this can
be revised.

Melanie
